content:
Has Optimization Culture Finally Maxxed on Maxxing?

How the gamification of living is ruining the game of life

Last week, Diary of a CEO podcaster Steven Bartlett shared that his life had essentially been ruined for three days after a night out with close friends, during which he indulged in—grab your pearls—two to three glasses of red wine. (Audible gasp!) 

The fallout? He reportedly slept poorly that night and even “podcasted worse” than usual the next day. A tragedy. 

Ordinarily, I would roll my eyes and move on with my day. But what happened next fascinated me: the internet collectively rolled its eyes even harder.

And the backlash felt…meaningful.

Over the last few years, a movement has taken hold across podcasts, social feeds, and pop culture, particularly among younger generations, that seems intent on measuring life based on wellness stats. A whole industry and culture has sprouted up with the supposed goal of helping us all reach our personally-assigned KPI. 

It started with Andrew Huberman’s cold plunges—a mostly harmless albeit excruciating gateway drug into wellness culture.

Then came the increasingly elaborate morning routines. The 5:30 am wake-ups with no snoozes, a giant glass of lemon water, 10 to 20 minutes of natural light exposure, no phone checks for at least an hour. Oh, and you can’t have a cup of coffee until 90 to 120 minutes after waking. 

Soon, our wrists and fingers were tracking our every bodily function with Oura Rings and Whoops. Then came protein-maxxing, sleep-maxxing, looksmaxxing, longevity-maxxing, and an endless parade of self-improvement projects that increasingly felt less like wellness and more like a competitive sport.

To optimize is human. To over-optimize is to miss what makes us human.

One of the internet’s most recognizable millennial influencers, Christina Najjar (better known as @Tinx), immediately weighed in on the Bartlett backlash:

“I think it’s really critical that we analyze what these ‘thought leaders’ are saying even in passing. My belief is that optimization culture has gotten out of control. It has robbed an entire generation of joy because of the demonization of fun.”

These trends have undeniably shaped broader consumer behavior, including, in part, the market corrections many of us in wine are navigating today. Alcohol has become framed not simply as indulgent, but as antithetical to peak performance itself.

Influencers and non-influencers added to Tinx’s reaction, revealing that perhaps we have finally reached peak maxxing. Max-maxxing, if you will.

Brand expert Ashwinn Bego weighed in on optimization culture to the tune of 1.5 million views. Dietitians like Jaclyn London told audiences that “wellness culture is the new diet culture.” Performance coach and researcher Brad Stulberg echoed similar concerns saying, “Actual excellence…demands resilience. It controls the controllables, no doubt. But it also ensures you don’t optimize yourself into fragility.” Even my favorite business news podcast, The Best One Yet, joined the discourse. 

Honestly? I have been saying this for years.

The crisis of isolation has never been more prevalent, and I suspect it is harming us far faster than an innocent glass of wine (or two) ever could.

Many of us eventually learn that trying to control every variable in life only ends in disappointment, anxiety, or misery, and yet we continue trying. We obsess over sleep scores, macros, steps, recovery data, peptides, glucose spikes, and perfect routines while quietly removing the very things that make life feel meaningful: mistakes, spontaneity, and real human connection.

As academic, author, and influencer Scott Galloway puts it:

“I think young people need to drink more. I don’t see drunkenness. I see togetherness… My advice to young people is to go out and drink more and make a series of bad decisions.”

His larger point being that people desperately need more connection, more strangers, more randomness, more missteps, more opportunities to bump into life and grow together. Because increasingly, many of us are becoming optimized for loneliness.

I come to an existential crossroads on the meaning of life more frequently than I’d like to admit. And when I do, I return again and again to one of my favorite episodes of one of my favorite shows: 30 Rock. Alec Baldwin’s legendary workaholic character, Jack Donaghy, has a near-death experience. Lying in a hospital bed, speaking to the equally iconic Liz Lemon (played by Tina Fey), he says:

“All the time I’ve been on this Earth I have only one regret: I should have worked more.”

The scene is funny because it so perfectly captures who Jack is at his core. But it is also funny because, of course, people famously have the opposite realization at the end of life that productivity is not everything.

Over the last few years, I’ve lost several people I love in a relatively short span of time. And you know what never came up in those final conversations? Their sleep score. Loss has one strange silver lining: it shines a very bright light on what actually matters. And I can tell you, without hesitation, that a spontaneous night out with friends, complete with two, or dare I say three, glasses of wine, will almost always make your life richer than a “perfect” day of work.

I’m relieved to see that perhaps, finally, more people are beginning to realize the same thing before it’s too late.

Jacqueline Strum

President & Publisher of Wine Enthusiast Media

title: Hate Buttery Chardonnay? Try These and Get Back to Us
summary:
With less acid courtesy of malolactic fermentation, these Chardonnays are smooth as butter—with just the right amount of bite.

The post Hate Buttery Chardonnay? Try These and Get Back to Us appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
While a crisp white is one of the wine world’s perennial favorites, we’re here to make the case for the decadent, rich universe of buttery Chardonnay. 

Few topics are as divisive in the wine landscape. Buttery Chardonnay’s infamously creamy quality is the product of malolactic fermentation, a technique involving introducing specific bacteria to wine. This addition converts tart malic acid into softer lactic acid, lowering the wine’s acidity and resulting in a rounder mouthfeel and a distinctively buttery flavor (thanks to Diacetyl, a byproduct of the process).

While we can agree that buttery Chard may be an acquired taste, some folks absolutely hate it. In our April 2023 print issue, Marshall Tilden III, the President of Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast, came out swinging against buttery Chard, albeit judiciously. “[Unlike] other, and more simple white varietals, the complexities of Chardonnay stand strong on their own without needing to soften, or transform, the flavor profile,” he said. Although malolactic conversion “can round out the acidity, softening the wine on the palate, I much prefer a Chard with searing acidity that has me craving a second sip.”

Team Buttery Chardonnay

On the other side of the spectrum, however, the style isn’t without its fans. 

“In a world that craves unadulterated wines more than ever, denying Chardonnay its natural inclination to undergo malolactic fermentation is an enological faux pas,” argues Matt Kettmann, Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large and reviewer of wines from California. “Why rely on modern technology to change the way this noble grape has been treated since Burgundian monks got a hold of it a millennium ago?”

WE Writer-at-Large Tom Capo, who reviews Sonoma County wines, is also a fan. “Sometimes, there’s no substitute for the decadence of a barrel-aged Chardonnay,” he says. “There’s a certain voluptuousness to well-made buttery Chardonnay that just hits different—especially during crab season. The velvety mouthfeel and rounded flavors of this style of Chardonnay accentuate the texture of succulent crab meat.”

Michael Alberty, a WE Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from the Pacific Northwest, similarly adores buttery Chards—with a caveat. “I look for Chardonnays that provide a bit of butter without making me feel like I’ve licked the bottom of a Land O’Lakes tub,” he says. “I’m in heaven if I find a Chardonnay that combines butter with elevated acidity.”

It’s All About Balance

This is all to say: Even those with a preference for buttery styles have their limits. What is most important, as with all wines, is a balanced flavor profile, and malolactic fermentation can play a crucial part in that balance.

“Listen, I love a racy Chardonnay more than most,” Kettmann says. “But the grape’s potential for screamingly high acidity—especially in cool-climate regions like California’s Central Coast—typically requires the smoother mouthfeel and warmer flavors that come from a bit of lactic acid. Like everything in wine, it’s all about finding that balance, and the absolute denial of one critical and natural tool in the winemaking process is the start of a dangerous journey. If you don’t like ML, you may just not like Chardonnay.”

If you’ve gotten this far in the story, we’re assuming you’re Team Buttery Chardonnay (or at least open-minded). To reward you, we’ve compiled a list of highly-rated bottles hailing from California, Australia, and beyond. These bottlings hit the sweet spot, splitting the difference between butter bomb and nose-searing acidity.

Rowen Wine Company 2023 Cooley Ranch Chardonnay (Sonoma County)

Punchy citrus, nectarine, lemongrass, river rock, and white flower aromas explode on the nose of this high elevation Chardonnay. The palate dances with lively acidity and energetic phenolic grip, fading into flavors of focused Meyer lemon, grilled Anjou pear, toasty hazelnut, a whisper of cinnamon, orange blossoms, and chopped lemon verbena along a mouthwatering finish. Stunning now, this will improve with patience. Editors’ Choice. 96 Points — Tom R. Capo

Inquire with the winery

La Crema 2023 Chardonnay (Los Carneros)

Aromas of citrus blossoms, white tea join flavors of spiced apple sauce with a dollop of caramel pudding through a palate washed clean by fresh acidity. Youthfully taut upon opening, this Chardonnay continues to reveal additional flavors and aromas with further sipping. 90 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$40 La Crema

Kosmic Kitty 2024 Chardonnay (California)

This wine is a fun and interesting selection because it uses cats. Imagine kicking back on the couch with your furry friend, enjoying a bright, aromatic Chardonnay that tastes like fresh, crisp apple, nutmeg, orange zest, with a touch of lightly toasted oak, and a mineral finish. Best Buy. 90 Points — Tonya Pitts

Inquire with the winery

Felton Road 2022 Block 2 Chardonnay (Central Otago)

Felton Road may be world renowned for their Pinots, but do not ignore their wonderful Chards. After all, if Pinot thrives in Central’s semi-arid, mountainous landscape, so too should Chardonnay. With a few years of bottle age, this is shining. There’s both vibrancy and depth of flavor: roasted cashew, lemon oil, truffle, beeswax, wet stone and a herbal edge. It’s mid weight, curvy; a toasty, flinty fringe around pithy, salted lemon fruit concentration to a long finish. Editors’ Choice. 96 Points — Christina Pickard

$50 Rye Brook Wine & Spirit Shop

Kumeu River 2024 Kumeu Village Chardonnay (Kumeu)

There’s an inviting lift of lemony goodness with a lick of nougat, cashew backbone, hight-toned, with whipped peaks of citrus and cream. A glycerine glide to the palate never feels heavy. In fact, freshness abounds, thanks to a laser beam of acidity that shoots right through the center of the tongue, keeping things tight and focused. Kumeu’s entry level Chard is a wonderful intro to the style, and this particular vintage is a total steal. Stock up, if you can. 94 Points — C.P.

$24 Flatiron Wines

Leeuwin Estate 2022 Art Series Chardonnay (Margaret River)

Straight from first sniff this reels you in. It’s opulent and saline with toasted hazelnuts, creme brûlée, hints of banana leaf, stone fruit, lemon, pineapple rind and a whisper of smoke. It’s beautifully balanced on the palate; quietly powerful; the toasted pastry flavors woven with tangy acidity and an alluring texture. It’s a complete wine now, but should also age gracefully for more than a decade. 96 Points — C.P.

$115 K&L Wines

Snake and Herring 2024 Corduroy Chardonnay (Margaret River)

There’s a lovely bright lemony lift to the nose of this Margaret River Chard, with melon, brine and toasty, cashew notes tucked behind. A line of salt-flecked acidity rushes straight down the middle of the tongue. Modern and approachable, yet also unmistakably “Margs” in style. 94 Points — C.P.

$40 Total Wine

More Chardonnay Coverage

  • Check out our essential guide to Chardonnay.
  • Then, take a deep dive into Chardonnayspeak.
  • If you’re ready to do some more shopping, these are our favorite Chardonnays from across the globe.
  • Want to stay stateside? Here are our best California Chardonnays.
  • Or, if you’re feeling the French vibe, these white Burgundies are exceptional.
  • And these Chablis are pure Cha-bliss.

title: ‘It’s Meant to Be a Good Time, Guys’: Elizabeth Banks Is Over Wine Snobbery
summary:
We spoke with Banks and Archer Roose CEO Marian Leitner-Waldman about being women in wine, industry woes, and which wines they (probably) drink in the 'Hunger Games.'

The post ‘It’s Meant to Be a Good Time, Guys’: Elizabeth Banks Is Over Wine Snobbery appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Actress and producer Elizabeth Banks began working as a restaurant server at age 15. In her hometown of Pittsfield, Massachusetts, as well as in cities like New York and San Francisco, Banks spent ten years pouring wines for high-end clientele, yet she still felt like she didn’t know enough about wine. “It was always intimidating to me as someone who was like … this feels expensive,” says Banks on the Wine Enthusiast Podcast. “I don’t want to say the wrong thing and embarrass myself… And who wants to feel that way when they’re interacting with any product?”

It’s why becoming a co-owner of Archer Roose Wines, along with CEO and founder Marian Leitner-Waldman, felt so personal to her. The canned wine brand’s mission is to make wine more accessible and approachable, without sacrificing quality. 

“We just don’t want to intimidate anyone out of the category,” says Banks. “We just want to be inviting. I don’t want anyone to think they don’t know anything about wine. Guess what? We tell you everything about it on the back of the can.” 

This May, Archer Roose launched a crisp unoaked Chardonnay from Côtes de Gascogne—i.e., a Chardonnay for people who think they don’t like Chardonnay. (The actress admits to having been one of those people before this launch.)

But Archer Roose’s ethos of accessibility stretches beyond just the wine itself. Both Banks and Leitner-Waldman believe that cans aren’t just a trend—they’re a solution to many of the problems plaguing the wine industry right now. Modern wine drinkers are gravitating towards smaller, lower-commitment portions. 

“We have to listen to what the consumer wants and rise to the occasion,” says Leitner-Waldman. “And the consumer has been unequivocal in its movement to cans across soda, beer, and spirits, which people never thought would happen.” 

We chatted with the Archer Roose co-owners about being women in wine, betting big on format, and which wines characters (probably) drink in the Hunger Games.

On Changing People’s Minds About Chardonnay

Marian Leitner-Waldman: “First and foremost, our whole identity has been about how do we bring a new generation into wine and how do we make wine accessible? And there’s very much certain varietals in this country where, as you do wine education and you kind of expand the tent, people are like, Oh, that’s not for me. That’s for my great aunt Flora, who likes to drink and taste it this way.

And that’s that’s very true of the American experience with Chardonnay, but globally it’s represented in so many different ways… I think that more and more younger wine drinkers want clean, crisp wines.”

Elizabeth Banks: “Yeah, I’ll say it even more plainly. Chardonnay, despite being one of the best-selling varietals in America, is not my favorite. And I am somebody who grew up being like, “that buttery, rich, sort of golden wine has just never worked for me.” Which isn’t to say that that’s not what maybe our consumers wanted, but it really was exciting to find something, a Chardonnay, that actually was crisper, was brighter, was lighter, which is what you want to drink out of a can, by the way… it’s a different experience than like that big goblet.”

On Falling in Love with Wine for the First Time

EB: “Right in my brain, just when you asked me that question, was a moment in France drinking rosé and being like, “Oh, this is what it’s supposed to be.” It’s supposed to be an experience. And it’s supposed to be about a sense of place. You know, there was something just so fully connected about those sips, that bottle, that night, and me being a European traveler and feeling like I was coming into my own as a person and feeling like I deserve wine. 

I can think of other moments like that too. I mean, wine has been for celebration and family and ritual and community for thousands and thousands of years. For us at Archer Roose, we are about that sense of experience, that sense of community, you know. Why do people drink wine? What do those occasions look like? And how do we meet consumers at those occasions, you know, like how do we enhance that occasion for them?”

MLW: “As usual, Elizabeth has just, I think, said it perfectly—wine is a vibe. And my earliest recollections and memories with wine are actually with Chardonnay. They’re with my mom and her best friend coming over on a Saturday night, rolling up our living room rug, putting on Bonnie Raitt and like, all of us kids, like everybody dancing, having a great time. And that is wine to me. It is the celebration.”

On Selling Canned Wine as ‘Replacement Pours’

MLW: “Something that Elizabeth and I have long talked about is that our brand can’t be built on convenience… or like canned wine as a category can’t be built on convenience. It has to be built on it being a replacement pour for a bottle. Not for a bottle that you’ve laid down for 10 years. That’s not what wine is for. But 97% of all wine that’s bought and sold in this country is less than two years old and consumed within 72 hours after purchase. So it’s for young and fresh wine, right? But it had to be a replacement pour for that.

We made the conscious choice to build in the on-premise because it was a way to trial and taste people on it and to demonstrate the quality. But it also ended up solving problems for our accounts. So it offered one-to-one inventory management. It offered that controlled pour. It made sure that you had no waste, right? You made money off the first can of wine you sold… as opposed to like, if you open up a bottle, you’re actually not making any money on that first pour. It’s only on the second. And so when you have that perfect alignment of consumer need and insight, solving problems for the trade, like that’s, I think, where magic can happen, but it doesn’t work unless the quality is there. And that’s really what we’ve staked our claim on.”

On the Wine Industry’s Biggest Problem Right Now

MLW: “I think the wine industry has created a problem of its own making. It is not consumer-centric. It is very attached to its own traditions and rituals without recognizing that over the 8,000 years of history of wine, those traditions have constantly evolved. And old traditions can coexist with new traditions.”

On Why Elizabeth Banks Got Involved 

EB: “Marian said, I am looking for not just a fellow female founder who’s, you know, irreverent and wants to disrupt an industry, which is something I do in entertainment already. But somebody who can tell the story of wine in a new way because we’re up against all of these traditions—you know, it’s not easy to disrupt a category that is this old.

It’s important to remember that when the cork started going out and people were getting twist tops, everyone was like, ‘This is the worst thing that’s ever happened to wine! How are we going to know what’s quality?’ And the wines with corks were telling the wines with twist tops that they were worse. And it’s like, ‘No, no, no, this is just a format change because we have to deal with sustainability. We have to deal with what is working and what’s not working supply chain wise, right?’

When Marian approached me, I was like, ‘I get it.’ The can is just the future of wine, format-wise. It solves the problems of sustainability, of waste management, of convenience for people. I just felt like, ‘Oh, this is literally just the future of wine.’”

On the Perils of Premiumization

MLW: “At some point, this industry decided that premium meant unaffordable, without recognizing that if you don’t have a price ladder, you inherently are not welcoming new people to the category.”

On Taking Women Wine Lovers Seriously 

MLW: “Women are by far the biggest cohort of daily drinkers in an industry that’s been built to cater to the 3% who are collectors and are overwhelmingly white and male. The women who are daily drinkers are across socioeconomic circumstances and races. They have different relationships with wine. And when we were marketed to, it’s often like, ‘This is your mommy juice or, you know, your jewel box or something.’ And we just wanted to create something smart that treated you like a person. 

But we recognize you probably can only have one because you’ve got to be up with your kids or you have to go to work and you have to manage 20 other things. So how do we be the brand for you? That’s not to say we don’t want men to drink our wine too. We love you all.”

On Why Banks Was Intimidated by Wine As a Young Person

EB: “I was very intimidated by wine as a young person, you know? And I was a server for ten years, by the way. So this is coming from someone who had to like, you know, pop the bottles and know about wine and serve wine in restaurants. In New York for a little bit, in San Francisco, in my hometown.

I started waitressing when I was 15, yeah. And I waitressed from 15 to 25, various different places. But pretty high end in New York for sure. And I say all that to say that even though I interacted with wine on that level, it was always intimidating to me as someone who was like… this feels expensive. I don’t know enough about it. I don’t want to say the wrong thing and embarrass myself… And who wants to feel that way when they’re interacting with any product?

… We just don’t want to intimidate anyone out of the category. That’s it. We just want to be inviting. I don’t want anyone to think they don’t know anything about wine. Guess what? We tell you everything about it on the back of the can. You can learn everything about this wine. Go to your next party and talk about it and build your status that way.”

On How Hollywood Prepared Her for the Wine World

EB: “In my entertainment company, we have to take the seed of an idea every time out and bring together all the right collaborators, put together the entire package, think about who the consumer is for it, sell the whole idea with marketing, and then put it out into the world. So that’s a skill set that as a producer of content I’ve been doing now for, you know, going on, I don’t know, almost 20 years. And so that skill set lent itself pretty one-to-one directly across when it came to building a wine brand and building a brand generally. You know, we have to build out Pitch Perfect with your, you know, ‘Who is this for? Who do I want to invite into this? Like, what is it?’ Et cetera. So there’s a lot of one-to-one there.

But at the very base level, it’s the storytelling, it’s about human connection, and how do we create a shared experience for the ‘audience,’ the consumers?… And just bringing that sense of irreverence and comedy, which I think is very sticky, to what I do. That’s who I am as an entertainer.”

On Why Canned Wine Is Not a Trend

MLW: “We have to listen to what the consumer wants and rise to the occasion of that consumer. And the consumer has been unequivocal in its movement to cans across soda, beer spirits, which people never thought would happen.” 

EB: “We don’t see this as a trend. We think that really this is, like, a behavioral solution for a generation that wants to drink less but drink more intentionally, whether that’s about moderation or occasion or convenience.”

On Which Wines Hunger Games Characters Drink

EB: “In District 12, they’ve got nothing… I mean, they’re drinking toilet wine. They’re making it themselves at home. But in District 2, we’re in the Capitol. The Capitol has it all. And they’re definitely walking around with their canned wine through the streets, for sure. Yeah… President Snow’s drinking our bubbly on the daily. Yeah.

Oh, [30 Rock’s] Avery Jessup would be thrilled about this upcoming Chardonnay launch, let me tell you… That is her wine.”

The Guests

Elizabeth Banks, Emmy-nominated actress, producer, writer, and director, joined Archer Roose Wines in 2021 as co-owner and Chief Creative Officer. A Massachusetts native, Banks is known for her roles in films and television series across genres, from the Hunger Games to Wet Hot American Summer, as well as her directorial work for Pitch Perfect 2, Cocaine Bear, and Charlie’s Angels.

Marian Leitner-Waldman is the founder and CEO of Archer Roose Wines, one of the fastest growing wine companies in the U.S. The brand’s Emmy-award winning commercials, starring business partner and actress Elizabeth Banks, have been lauded by the San Francisco Chronicle as the “most innovative wine marketing around.” Marian was one of Wine Industry Advisor’s “Most Inspiring People in Wine” in 2025 and was honored as one of the 2025 Women in Beverage by the Beverage Information Group.

More About the Podcast

The Wine Enthusiast Podcast is your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. You can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Visit the podcast homepage for more episodes and transcripts.

Speakers: Maria Yagoda, Marian Leitner-Waldman, Elizabeth Banks

In this episode, we talk about wine’s lingering snobbery problem, why the future of the industry belongs in a can, and how they are navigating a shifting consumer landscape by meeting wine drinkers where they are. Plus, the Hunger Games actress reveals which wine she thinks they drink in District 12 and the Capitol and how her career in Hollywood prepped her for the beverage industry. Listen to learn more. 

Well, welcome. It’s so lovely to have you both, Elizabeth and Maryanne. And first, before we even kind of get into it, you’re coming out with a Chardonnay and despite being such a ubiquitous and popular grape, there are still so many misconceptions around it, especially in America. So I’m curious, what are you kind of hoping to debunk about all of these Chardonnay stereotypes that people have?

Marian Leitner-Waldman

Well, I think there’s a few different ways that I want to answer this. So first and foremost, our whole identity has been about how do we bring a new generation into wine and how do we make wine accessible? And there’s very much certain varietals in this country where it’s as you do wine education and you kind of expand the tent, people are like, “Oh, that’s not for me. That’s for my great aunt Flora who likes to drink and taste it this way.” And so what we just really wanted to do was create… And that’s very true of the American experience with Chardonnay, but globally it’s represented in so many different ways. And a huge core component of archeroose wines is about how do you explore the world of wine? And so we went to Cote de Gascon to France and we met this amazing female winemaker, Delphine, and she has just this delicious, clean, crisp Chardonnay that is just one beautiful example of way this very expressive grape presents itself.

And so we’re thrilled to bring it. And I think that more and more younger wine drinkers want clean, crisp wines, but even within that, there’s so many different expressions and flavors to bring out. And that’s what we were really set out to do.

Elizabeth Banks

Yeah. I’ll say it even more plainly. Chardonnay, despite being one of the best selling varietals in America is not my favorite. And I am somebody who grew up being like that buttery, rich sort of golden wine has just never worked for me. It’s a different experience than that big goblet. I really think our Chardonnay speaks to people who sort of like that Chardonnay, but are not won over by it. And I’m telling you, this one is going to win them over because this is the Chardonnay for people who don’t love it like me, but get to that picnic or want to bring something that they know people are going to love in the backyard picnic. This is the Chardonnay for that person.

Love that. And you mentioned Aunt Flora who might have had Chardonnay in her day. And I was recently talking to some friends. I was curious what people thought of when I said California Chardonnay and it was oak, oak, butter, butter, butter, mom, mom, my mom, oak, my mom, butter. And I’m curious thinking about what were your first introductions to wine in general and what made you fall in love with wine? I’m guessing it was not a Chardonnay. And I’m just curious what your first aha moment was with loving wine.

EB

Right in my brain, just when you asked me that question was a moment in France drinking rose and being like, “Oh, this is what it’s supposed to be. It’s supposed to be an experience and it’s supposed to be about a sense of place.” There was something just so fully connected about those sips, that bottle, that night where I was like this and me being a European traveler and feeling like I was coming into my own as a person and feeling like I deserve wine. Do you know what I mean? And I can think of other moments like that too. I mean, wine, it’s for celebration and family and ritual and community for thousands and thousands of years. But I think for us at Archer Roose, we are about that sense of experience, that sense of community. Why do people drink wine? What do those occasions look like?

And how do we meet consumers at those occasions? How do we enhance that occasion for them? And for me, that’s when drinking wine as a younger person, that’s what brought me to it. That sense of occasion and fun and community and the girls are all together or the brunch is brunching. You know what I mean? And so how do we create that feeling for people when they’re drinking and having fun with each other?

MLW

And as usual, Elizabeth has just, I think, said it perfectly. Wine is a vibe. And my earliest recollections and memories with wine are actually with Chardonnay. They’re with my mom and her best friend coming over on a Saturday night, rolling up our living room rug, putting on Bonnie Raitt and all of us kids, everybody dancing, having a great time. And that is wine to me. It is the celebration. It is this community. And as I got older and went on my own journey with wine, there have definitely been a handful of moments where I’m like, “Oh, now I really love the taste. Now I can really appreciate the structure.” But first and foremost, it’s got to be a state of mind and a feeling.

MY

Right. And you mentioned on – premise has been big for Archer Roose Wines and you’re in a lot of sports stadiums. And when did that kind of click that that was going to be a really important part of the business?

MLW

Well, it actually goes back to something that Elizabeth and I have long talked about, which is that our brand can’t be built on convenience. Canned wine as a category can’t be built on convenience. It has to be built on it being a replacement pour for a bottle, not for a bottle that you’ve laid down for 10 years. That’s not what wine is for, but 97% of all wine that’s bought and sold in this country is less than two years old and consumed within 72 hours after purchase. So it’s for young and fresh wine, right? But it had to be a replacement pour for that. And as we were building the credibility of the space, wanting to be scrappy and wanting to tap into this vibe and this feeling, we made the conscious choice to build in the on – premise because it was a way to trial and taste people on it and to demonstrate the quality, but it also ended up solving problems for our accounts.

So it offered one-to-one inventory management. It offered that control pour. It made sure that you had no waste. You made money off the first can of wine you sold as opposed to if you open up a bottle, you’re actually not making any money on that first pour. It’s only on the second. And so when you have that perfect alignment of consumer need and insight, solving problems for the trade, that’s I think where magic can happen, but it doesn’t work unless the quality is there. And that’s really what we’ve staked our claim on.

MY

Yeah. And I’m sure with the pandemic also there had to be some tweaks to the approach. And as I was doing my research for this interview, Archer Roose has been on airplanes and in restaurants. And even now kind of beyond that, as consumer tastes are shifting and the industry is facing its own headwinds, how do you feel like the RTV space is now super crowded and it seems like Archer Roose has still found a way to kind of break free of the big, big crowd and stand out. So I’m curious how you approach that as the landscape feels like it’s constantly shifting.

MLW

I think there’s a lot to unpack in that question.

EB

I agree. Which part do you want to take?

MLW

I think what I want to tackle first is the wine industry piece and the wine industry changing. I don’t believe that I think the wine industry has created a problem of its own making. It is not consumer centric. It is very attached to its own traditions and rituals without recognizing that over the 8,000 years of history of wine, those traditions have constantly evolved. And they can also, old traditions can coexist with new traditions. But the bottom line about our brand is that we decided that we wanted to be a part of people’s lives on a daily basis. And so that meant we had to lean into format so that they could just have that glass. They just lean into not just moderation, but recognizing that people don’t live in intergenerational homes anymore.You need to make it so that it’s easy and accessible. The second is that you have to recognize that the way people live and spend their time has evolved.

Experiences are so much more important and cans have been embraced across categories. And so we need to be a part of that otherwise we will be displaced by a light beer or an RTD. But none of that matters if the quality is not there. And this is why we partnered with Cornell since 2019. And in the process, we’ve actually developed our own suite of patents to protect the quality of our wine and to make sure that this could be a true replacement pour. And you don’t have to take my word for it. We actually just won the Sommelier Choice Awards for our Sauvignon Blanc where we are the wine of the year from Chile. Not in a can, but full stop.

MY

That’s amazing.

EB

So I’ll jump in right there because that’s a perfect example of the storytelling that we sought out to do when we first partnered. Marian said, I am looking for not just a fellow female founder who’s irreverent and wants to disrupt an industry, which is something I do in entertainment already, but somebody who can tell the story of wine in a new way because we’re up against all of these traditions, because we’re up against… It’s not easy to disrupt a category that is this old. It’s important to remember, I think, that when the cork started going out and people were getting twist tops, everyone was in a… It was like, this is the worst thing that’s ever happened to wine. How are we going to know what’s quality? And the wines with corks were telling the wines with twist tops that they were worse than the wine.

And it’s like, no, no, no, this is just a format change because we have to deal with sustainability. We have to deal with what is working and what’s not working supply chain wise. To me, when Marion approached me, I was like, I get it. The can is just the future of wine in format wise. It solves the problems of sustainability, of waste management, of convenience for people. I just felt like, oh, this is literally just the future of wine. Now let’s talk about how we have to change the storytelling because when you tell older people it’s wine in the can, they immediately think it’s lesser quality. So that’s the storytelling that we’re doing on a daily basis, which is making sure that we’re in blind taste tests. We’re not just in canned wine categories. We want to be the tide that brings up all the boats behind us.

We want to be first and foremost when people think about canned wine. They think about Archer Roose because it’s delicious. Period. That’s it. It’s just really good and it fits their lifestyle. But that’s been the challenge and the storytelling challenge that I’ve taken on of us sort of saying, let’s not take this all too seriously. Who are we all kidding? We’re not snobby wine drinkers talking to sommeliers and ordering wine in cases from France. We want to enjoy wine tomorrow on the soccer field next to the pool while we’re visiting friends.That’s it. Let’s actually talk about wine in the way that consumers actually consume it to have fun. And so to me, that storytelling element, that’s what’s been most disruptive in the category. It goes along with our format, but they had to go together.

MY

Yeah. And I’m sure it’s an ongoing process because there’s something so specific about the wine industry. I’ve worked in food, media and kind of all over the place, but the wine industry is so rooted in these traditions and it does feel like there are so many barriers to entry. There aren’t so many excellent entry point wines in the way there are with beer. There’s an affordability problem for a lot of new of age drinkers.

MLW

The affordability piece, at some point, this industry decided that premium meant unaffordable without recognizing that if you don’t have a price later, you inherently are not welcoming new people to the category.

EB

Yeah.

MY

And everyone’s, oh, Gen Z, millennials are drinking wrong. They’re not doing it the right way. And no, you got to move quickly. You got to meet people where they are. And it’s striking to me also to be chatting with two women about this because I feel like women are often very underserved by the wine industry. So I’m curious what that’s been like for you guys.

MLW

Well, first hats off for the pun.

MY

Thank you. Yes, it was planned. Very planned.

MLW

I mean, this is something that Elizabeth and I talk about a lot. And I think she brings this up and reminds me often as I sometimes fall into sort of my industry perspective, but women are by far the biggest cohort of daily drinkers in an industry that’s been built to cater to the 3% who are collectors and are overwhelmingly white and male.

The women who are daily drinkers are across socioeconomic circumstances and races. They have different relationships with wine. But when we are marketed to, it’s often like, this is your mommy juice or your jewel box or something. And we just wanted to create something smart that treated you like a person. I mean, who do you want to have a drink with? You want to have a drink with Elizabeth Banks? She’s everyone’s favorite hang. And so a big part of this was about how do we take what’s actually relevant to women? And one core part is that we have a lot on our plate. You deserve a treat at the end of the day, which is your glass of wine, but we recognize you probably can only have one because you’ve got to be up with your kids and you have to go to work and you have to manage 20 other things.

So how do we be the brand for you?

But that’s not to say we don’t want men to drink our wine too. We love you all. Yes.

EB

Yeah, of course. But I do think there’s one of the other things I thought when we first started talking about my first interactions with wine as a young drinker, definitely that luckily the first thing in my mind was being improvence. But another thing I would say is I was very intimidated by wine as a young person. And I was a server for 10 years by the way. So this is someone kind of who had to pop the bottles and know about wine and serve wine in restaurants. Yeah. I was a server for 10 years.

In New York for a little bit in San Francisco, in my hometown. I started waitressing when I was 15. Yeah. And I waitressed from 15 to 25 different, various different places, but pretty high end in New York for sure. And I say all that to say that even though I interact with wine on that level, it was always intimidating to me as someone who was like, “This feels expensive. I don’t know enough about it. I don’t want to say the wrong thing and embarrass myself. I don’t know how to order it off the… ” It was just, I think it put me on a back foot. And who wants to fee that way when they’re interacting with any product? And so it’s just sort of like, “Wow, the wine industry, there was a snobbery to it. ” And so I still think when I interact with people now, there’s still those people that are like, “I would not drink this.

There’s a status obsessed symbol of certain wines, et cetera. That’s just not our company at all. We are trying to create wine drinkers that maybe get there and maybe want that and are not, but we just don’t want to intimidate anyone out of the category. That’s it. We just want to be inviting. I don’t want anyone to think, I don’t know anything about wine. Guess what? We tell you everything about it on the back of the can. You can learn everything about this wine, go to your next party and talk about it and build your status that way and seem cool that way. And so that’s the other thing that really excited me about what we were doing with Archer Roose. Luxury wine in a can for everybody accessible, not intimidating, just have fun with it. It’s meant to be a good time guys.

MY

And I also love that you worked as a server for 10 years because I feel like you had a front row seat to a lot of these attitudes about wine. And I’m also curious, working in entertainment, if there are any learnings from that space that you feel like you’re able to bring over here or kind of vice versa.

EB

Yeah. I mean, I think, well, for sure I would say as a producer, writer, director, we’re constantly as a company in my entertainment company, we have to take the seat of an idea every time out and bring together all the right collaborators, put together the entire package, think about who the consumer is for it, sell the whole idea with marketing and then put it out into the world. So that’s a skill set that as a producer of content, I’ve been doing now for going on, I don’t know, almost 20 years. And so that skillset lent itself pretty one to one directly across when it came to building a wine brand and building a brand generally. You have to build out pitch perfect with your who is this for? Who would I want to invite into this? What is it? Et cetera. So there’s a lot of one to one there.

But at the very base level, it’s the storytelling about human connection and how do we create a shared experience for the quote audience, the consumers. And just bringing that sense of irreverence and comedy, which I think is very sticky to what I do. That’s who I am as an entertainer. And I think it’s that side of myself is what I’m bringing pretty fundamentally to Archeroos because we are trying to be disruptive in a category that did take itself really seriously. And we think really desperately needed a lighter touch to sort of connect with consumers as you’ve been saying where they are. And so I just feel like whether I’m stepping into this fun character of Marian’s thorn on her side, sidekick, co-owner of Archer Roose Wines, I love being that character to her and annoying the hell out of her. I like being that character, but it’s really just about, we’re just trying to create what are some memorable storytelling elements that we can bring to a category that has really never done what we’re doing.

We just want to make luxury wine genuinely fun.

MY

And so looking ahead, what’s exciting to you about what you guys might be getting into in the future? Does it occur to you that there might be a brand new format that we haven’t even conceived of yet that’s going to happen? When you look out at the horizon of Archeroos, what are you excited about?

MLW

Well, there’s a few different things that we’re excited about. First, we’re excited about what’s working. 25% of our growth comes from just increased velocity from accounts that have been with us year over year. So that means that people are trying us and then every year those accounts are growing. And we’ve seen that across the wholesale channel. And then of course through our partnership with JetBlue and now through our new partnership with Breeze Airways. We got one projection for the year and our partnership’s a couple months old and we’ve already exceeded it. So I am going to touch on something that you said just now, which is I know that there is a thought in this industry that there’s a recognition that we have to find an alternative to the 750 milliliter glass bottle, but maybe that alternative format doesn’t exist yet.

I would push back on that and say instead, we have to listen to what the consumer wants and rise to the occasion of that consumer. And the consumer has been unequivocal in its movement to cans across soda, beer, spirits, which people never thought would happen. And the reason why they’re saying that they’re raising this question about format is because when the strategics got into canned wine, it was a failure because they didn’t pay attention to how canning wine is different than bottling wine. And that was a big question that the brewers grappled with in the early 2000s cracked because of consumer demand. And so my challenge to the industry is to say, let’s actually give the consumer what they want and instead ask us how to be able to deliver a dangerously delicious glass of wine to that consumer for whenever they want it. And in the process we can unlock new occasions and I truly believe we’ll welcome a new Renaissance era into the industry.

MY

Love to hear that because sometimes I don’t hear… I mean, often I don’t hear that actually because I talk to a lot of people in the industry and there’s such a sense of doom and gloom sometimes. And seeing such a big success story and leaning into what the consumer has been telling you unequivocally what they want and are interested in is refreshing. So I love to hear that.

MLW

Now we just need the industry to partner with us in order to help accelerate I think this movement. Because that’s fundamentally what it is.

EB

I think movement is such a good word. I know we’ve talked about how this, we don’t see this as a trend. We think that really this is, it’s like a behavioral solution for a generation that wants to drink less, but drink more intentionally. Whether that’s about moderation or occasion or convenience. I think that’s what’s been really exciting for us is seeing and then how many solutions we provide for people who want to have a fun glass of wine. Yeah.

MY

Right. And I think that also being able to cater to and be mindful of those drinkers who do want to drink less, they’re still invited to the party too. Rather than be like, “Why aren’t they drinking more? This is ruining everything.”

MLW

Exactly. And the reality too is that we’re still comparing ourselves to what the industry did in 2020 when we were all drinking our faces off and that was not sustainable. So this is really just saying like, listen, we are all healing still I think from COVID in a lot of ways. And one of the reasons why we have been so dedicated to the on – premise is, well, there’s the business reason, which is that our format allows us to go to accounts where wine has never played before and be a part of consumer stories in a way where like wine didn’t show up. And that is a massive financial opportunity. But then there’s the emotional because ultimately we’re all in wine because we are delusional romantic suckers at the end of the day and it’s about connection. And for us, the on – premise is about community and being able to scale our community as fast as we can. And that’s because I think we’re still healing from being isolated.

EB

It is a giant collective trauma.

MLW

Yes.

EB

Totally. And Marian and I really connected during that collective trauma. And it was drinking the wine. I’ve tell this story, but it was like I had moved my family to Utah in the pandemic because we could be outside a lot. And I was floating down a river, drinking the wine, the bubbly dipped in the river and keeping the can cold. And it was like, this is amazing.

MY

And is that how you got connected? You were like, “I have to be in touch with this. “

EB

Yeah. We got connected through some business associates, one of whom texted me and said, “I have this woman reaching out about canned wine and I feel like don’t take this the wrong way. That’s totally you. ” And I though, “You know me so well, friend in business.” Yes, it is amazing. It’s amazing.

MY

Oh my gosh. Yeah. I think the industry is being reborn a million times over, especially with the pandemic and love chatting with two women about this. And so I think also going back to the Chardonnay thing, it’s like, “My mom drank this Chardonnay.” There’s also been this association in the industry that things that women like are bad.

MLW

Completely.

EB

Don’t get us started.

MLW

Yeah. Don’t get us started on this. But the point is, is that they control more purchasing power than ever. So give the woman what they want.

EB

That’s how you make the money.

MY

Yeah. They’re going to show up and spend. So stop ignoring them. Final question because my coworkers really wanted to know what type of wine do you think they drink in the district? I’m so sorry. 

EB

First of all, in District 12, they’re like, they got nothing. I mean, they’re making – Toilet wine? Yeah, they’re drinking toilet wine. Exactly. They’re making it themselves at home. But in district two, we’re in the Capitol. The Capitol has it all. The Capitol has it all and they’re walking around with their canned wine through the streets for sure. Yeah.

MLW

Absolutely.

EB

Yeah. Love that. For sure. President Snow’s drinking our bubbly on the daily.

MY

Love to hear it. Oh, one more. This is for me. This is a for me thing. What about Avery Jessup? What is her go – to?

EB

Oh, Avery Jessup would be thrilled about this upcoming Chardonnay launch. Let me tell you her wine. It’s like we made it for Republicans. Let’s just say that. We were looking for a wine for our Republican lady friends. This is our wine. Oh my God.

MY

That’s so funny. Yes. I love the storytelling that you bring to the table, it’s amazing.

MY

How do you feel about canned wine? Do you think the wine industry is doing a good enough job meeting consumers where they are? We’d love to know. You can email us your comments and questions at podcast@wineenthusiast.com. Remember, you can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. You can also go to wineenthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts. I’m Maria Yagoda. Thanks for listening.

The post ‘It’s Meant to Be a Good Time, Guys’: Elizabeth Banks Is Over Wine Snobbery appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot
summary:
Quieter than the more-famous Reims, Épernay has an eclectic food and drink scene that the region’s winemakers love.

The post This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Champagne’s identity has long been split between two cities: Reims and Épernay. Reims has the grand Gothic squares and urban edge, while Épernay is quieter, shaped by vineyard slopes and the Marne River.

In Épernay, the connection to the land behind some of the world’s most coveted wines feels far more direct. A patchwork of vineyards appears almost as soon as you exit the train station, and some of Champagne’s most celebrated wine villages sit within just a few miles of town—close enough to reach easily by bicycle.

For anyone who has returned to Épernay regularly over the past five years, the city’s transformation is hard to miss. Not so long ago, a walk along the chalk-white Avenue de Champagne—which forms part of Champagne’s UNESCO-listed landscape—could feel surprisingly sleepy. Today, the doors of its prestigious Champagne houses are open wide, with each looking for new ways to impress visitors.

On that same avenue is the recently opened Musée du Vin de Champagne et d’Archéologie Régionale, set inside the eclectic former mansion of the Perrier-Jouët family. The modern, interactive museum offers a concise way to trace 2,000 years of regional history while also unpacking the technical side of Champagne production.

Épernay is gaining new energy, with ambitious restaurants, wine bars, and four- and five-star hotels opening year after year. Champagne, of course, is not a single style but many: chalky and electric from Avize, generous and red-fruited from Bouzy, vinous and earthy from Les Riceys. That range calls for an equally eclectic food scene. As the winemakers who know Épernay best make clear, there is no shortage of places to experience it—from an apéritif before dinner to a long meal after touring the historic chalk cellars.

Where to Eat in Épernay

The town has its share of beloved dining institutions, and La Grillade Gourmande is one of them. Lyon-born chef Christophe Bernard, who worked with the legendary Paul Bocuse, moves from table to table with his servers, presenting signature dishes such as pigeon with foie gras in puff pastry, venison in pepper sauce, and sea bass with fennel. Everything is cooked in the homey dining room, over an open wood-burning fireplace.

“For me, this is Épernay’s standout bistronomic restaurant. I love the wood-fired cooking, which brings tremendous character to the dishes, as well as the chef’s distinctive personality that comes through on every plate,” says Sacha Geoffroy of Champagne Geoffroy, based in Aÿ.

Her father, Jean-Baptiste Geoffroy, once told me about Chez Max, another lively institution on the banks of the Marne that has been welcoming guests for nearly 80 years. At nearby tables, locals from Champagne recall coming here as children and now return with children of their own. Franck Benard and his wife deliver classic French service with real flair, turning tableside steak tartare into a small performance and theatrically presenting a platter of local cheeses. The cooking is generous, built around seasonal ingredients and deeply flavored sauces. If you see the escargot pie on the menu, don’t hesitate.

Before turning to the city’s more cosmopolitan side, two more addresses for classic French cooking come recommended by Odilon de Varine, cellar master at Champagne Gosset, based in Épernay.

“L’Oben is a very recent addition to Épernay and remains something of a hidden address,” he says. “With only a handful of tables, it offers an intimate atmosphere and serves classic French cuisine executed with great finesse.” Think crisp tarte fine with Bouzy Grand Cru snails, followed by farm-raised pork so tender it spent eight hours getting there.

For a more refined dining experience, de Varine recommends Why Not, especially before an evening at the theater. “The restaurant works with regional ingredients but approaches them with the precision and ambition of a gastronomic restaurant,” he says. The room pairs warm wood, high ceilings, muted tones, and low lighting, while the menu leans into French comfort dishes: think aged Comté ravioli gratin and half-roasted Camembert with Marc de Champagne to start, followed by knife-cut Charolais beef tartare.

A break from the classics comes at the hip, locals-loved Sacré Bistro, where the menu includes a signature burger with melted Comté, alongside other seasonal comfort plates. “The wine list there is one of the most interesting in the region,” says Geoffroy. “You’ll find everything from outstanding Champagne gems to rarer wines from around the world that can be difficult to find elsewhere.”

She also recommends stopping by Cook’in, where chef Guillaume Rousseau brings together French aesthetics and Thai flavors, with dishes such as duck foie gras paired with green apple and ginger confit, and grilled beef tournedos with red curry sauce. “It’s a rather unique address in Épernay,” Geoffroy says. “The Thai cuisine is consistently well-executed, and the contrast between these vibrant Asian flavors and the carefully curated Champagne selection works remarkably well.”

The Asian thread continues at L’Eurasienne, a family-run restaurant where bò lúc lắc is served alongside excellent pizzas. It is a particular favorite of Étienne Calsac, a grower-producer based in Avize. “What I appreciate most is that the wine selection is thoughtful and effective, offering good choices without being overly complicated,” he says. Think bún with lemongrass-grilled pork, or a pizza topped with white asparagus, and 24-month-aged Parmigiano Reggiano.

Where to Drink in Épernay

On Avenue de Champagne, one address stands apart from the polished tasting lounges of the Champagne houses. At Bar Brut, a small family-run bar, you won’t find the Grandes Marques. Instead, the focus is on grower friends such as Vincent Charlot, Louise Brison, and Franck Pascal, whose stories the team is happy to share while filling your glass to the brim. Take a seat in the carefully tended inner garden and, after paying Paris prices, prepare to get emotional: a glass of 2017 vintage blanc de blancs costs €7.50.

For an evening drink, Calsac recommends Le Pif Bar. The atmosphere is pleasingly countercultural, with barrels in place of tables and darts played over a glass of Champagne. “It’s a relatively new wine bar that opens only at night and has quickly become part of the local wine scene,” he says.

When Geoffroy meets friends for a glass, she often chooses 75cl, right in the heart of Épernay. “I really like this place for its dual identity as both a wine shop and a wine bar,” she says. “The selection is always thoughtfully curated, with excellent Champagne producers alongside strong choices from other regions. The atmosphere is simple, warm, and welcoming.”

More Travel Coverage

  • Planning a trip to Napa? Make sure to avoid these common travel mistakes.
  • Here is everywhere to eat, drink, and stay in Seoul.
  • Next time you’re in Italy, skip Venice, and head to Friuli-Venezia Giulia for a slew of culinary gems.
  • WE Executive Editor Maria Yagoda went to a wellness resort—for the wine.

title: Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated?
summary:
Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, but growth has slowed to a trickle. Some are rethinking the entire model.

The post Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Key Points

  • Direct-to-consumer wine club growth slowed to 2% in 2024, down from 11% in 2018.
  • 58% of consumers paused at least one recurring subscription service in the past year.
  • Wineries are shifting toward flexible memberships and exclusive releases to boost engagement.

Not long after husband-and-wife duo Chris and Kathryn Hermann founded Double Zero Wines in Carlton, Oregon, in 2015, they started a wine club because, well, they thought they had to.

“We were looking at what other people were doing, especially in the Willamette Valley and California and said, ‘OK, we’ll need a 10% case discount, we’ll ship wine twice a year’—we checked all the boxes,” says co-proprietor and general manager, Kathryn. 

Being part of a winemakers’ collective, the Hermanns don’t have access to the onsite tasting room, which often facilitates community with members. In 2020, as Double Zero settled deeper into the fine wine realm, the Hermanns traded subscriptions for another obligatory model: biannual private allocations. But reserving the best bottles for the biggest spenders likewise felt “icky,” Kathryn says. About six months ago, the couple abandoned clubs altogether in favor of simply putting their wine up for sale on their e-commerce platform. 

“Why have we conceived of these weird ways of buying things in the wine world?” Kathryn says. “If someone loves your wine, they want to go online and buy however much or little they want.”

Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, accounting for 25% of sales channels in 2025, per Silicon Valley Bank’s Direct to Consumer Wine Report. But growth in recent years has slowed to a trickle, averaging 2% in 2024, down from 5% the year before and 11% in 2018. Some are rethinking the model—trading biannual bottle shipments for experiential discounts or merchandise. Others have abandoned it for more personalized approaches to fostering loyalty, like splashy, in-person wine release drops.

“People change—the things they want, the way they live,” says Jen Cossey, general manager of the stalwart Brooks Wine in Amity, Oregon. “Even if you have the same members for ten years, you need to evolve because your customers are. If you’re not speaking to everybody, you’ll get left behind.”

Subscription Fatigue and Aging Drinkers

There’s a lot conspiring against wine clubs beyond soaring online shipping costs. U.S. tasting room visits—long the genesis of club memberships—have slipped amid a broader slowdown in alcohol sales. Generational changes are afoot, as wine-loving Baby Boomers age out of loading cases into their trunks and into retirement homes. Some Gen Xers are cutting back on or abstaining from wine altogether for health or financial reasons as they advance through middle age. 

Gen Z, meanwhile, tends to treat wine as an accessory to experiences rather than something to accumulate. It’s also hard to ignore the effects of subscription fatigue in the era of recurring autopayments—for streaming services and meal kits, Substack newsletters, beauty boxes, and coffee. Some 58% of consumers have paused a subscription within the past year, according to Chargebee’s 2025 Global Consumer Insights Report. Not to mention the democratization of wine discovery and access in the online era.

“We have to think more about how people, especially younger people, shop online and discover stuff rather than who’s going to walk in the door of my tasting room, fill out a form, and give me their mailing address,” says Issamu Kamide, cofounder and CEO of Wonderwerk, a natural wine brand in Santa Cruz, California.

From the get go, Wonderwerk leaned into broad distribution and pop-up events, in part because the winery didn’t have a physical space in the early days. Kamide and his cofounder, winemaker Andy Lardy, have spent the past nine years building the wholesale business and making easy-drinking, quality natural wines at scale. They’re methodically evolving Wonderwerk to resemble a coveted consumer good like sneakers rather than an agricultural product deemed valuable through unapproachable bona fides like vintages.  “We want to make it so exciting that people come rushing for it,” says Lardy. 

Capitalizing on consumers’ learned expectation for “new, new, new all the time,” owing to SKU proliferation in the consumer packaged goods (CPG) world, Wonderwerk treats wine releases more like streetwear or album drops, pairing them with live tasting events and thumping DJ sets. They’re always toying with creative ways to engage with their customers, from limited-release labels with up-and-coming artists to an online party portal that will enable consumers to order wines customized to the party they’re throwing. 

As Wonderwerk’s national reach expands, it’s been recruiting like-minded proxies like Jahmahl Pardner, who started Chicago-based wine club Voice Wine. Pardner focuses on low-intervention indie growers and hosts regular tasting events in his garage. A recent satellite party showcasing Wonderwerk’s latest Chicago release sold out of 120 tickets in 24 hours. 

“The event was packed, and we got feedback from him about what people liked, what people were saying,” Kamide says. 

Personalization Over Customization

Increasingly, it seems, loyalty comes with meeting customers where they’re at rather than forcing the same old model on them. For Kathryn at Double Zero, that means being on call for a client seeking a specific vintage of Pinot Noir by a specific date so he can fly with it to his wine club in Switzerland. “That’s how I take care of them.” 

For Brooks, whose 2,500-strong wine club membership largely comprises Millennials and Gen Xers, it means broadening the notion of “membership,” rather than abandoning it altogether. After subscription numbers dropped in consecutive years owing to consumers’ financial and health concerns, the winery debuted Brooks with Benefits, a choose-your-own adventure club riff. Members’ quarterly dues appear as a credit in their accounts, which they can put toward wine shipments, food in the tasting room, events like cooking classes, and merchandise. 

“For people who say, ‘I can’t be part of a wine club because I have too much wine,’ it solves for that,” Cossey says. “You’re not necessarily committing to wine. We’re inviting people to be part of the community in a way that makes sense for them.”

Last summer, the winery also joined forces with seven likeminded, sustainable indie wineries, all from different appellations, to roll out the Atlas Wine Collective. This multistate club share offers perks like complimentary tastings and discounts on wine after identifying members through POS-developed technology. In other words, a member at one is treated like a member at all. The group aims to expand with a bluesky goal of being in every state. 

Indeed, as Kathryn points out, this disconcerting moment has an upside in that it’s forcing wineries to think outside the (subscription) box. 

“We’re in a new era of wine buying; everything has been scrambled,” she says. “A lot of winemakers are scared, but I think this is a tremendous opportunity to change the way we sell and communicate to our customers.”

title: These 12 Chablis Are Pure Cha-Bliss
summary:
Chardonnay is produced around the world, but the varietal arguably shines brightest in Chablis. Here's why.

The post These 12 Chablis Are Pure Cha-Bliss appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Driving into Chablis, the town at the center of Burgundy’s northernmost region, the road descends into what feels like another world. It may be just 10 miles off the A6 highway that runs from Paris to the South of France, but quiet, wine-dominated Chablis is a step back in time.

Gabled houses with slate roofs surround a massive church. Narrow streets are full of the essentials of French country life. Bakers, butchers, and cafés flourish despite the existence of modern supermarkets.

And then, there are the wineries. You find them down every street, where they sit behind discreet doors that lead to interior courtyards and underground cellars. You almost can’t escape the vines—they’re what Chablis lives and breathes.

What Makes Chablis Special?

While not explicitly written on the label, all wines from Chablis are made from 100% Chardonnay. No other grape is allowed in the four Chablis Appellation d’Origine Contrôlée (AOC), and no one has seen the need to change that. It is here, in the northernmost region of Burgundy, that some believe the grape is expressed in its “purest” form.

Like all of Burgundy’s regions, Chablis has a legally enshrined hierarchy based on the quality of the land and soil. The cool-climate region is actually closer to Champagne’s vineyards than southerly sections of Burgundy, which makes vineyard orientation critical. South-, southeast-, or southwest-facing vineyards experience more sun exposure, grow better fruit, and generally make better wines. Proximity to the Serein river also adds a few extra degrees of warmth, which can result in added richness.

There are four levels in the Chablis appellation hierarchy: Petit Chablis, Chablis, Chablis Premier Cru, and Chablis Grand Cru. The latter three are the most lauded, especially where vineyards are planted on Kimmeridgian soil. Named after the English village of Kimmeridge where it was discovered, this chalk soil dates to the Jurassic period, and its composition of fossilized sea creatures gives Chablis its steely or flinty character.

With its cool climate and unique soils, Chablis expresses the terroir with an entirety that’s impossible to find in warmer regions, even its great Burgundian neighbors to the south. In the aging process, oak is sometimes used on higher-end bottles, but most Chablis producers encourage the purity of their fruit with steel-tank fermentation. As a result, tropical, rounded, oaky, or buttery are seldom used as descriptors in Chablis. Instead, these wines are crisp, textured, and acid-driven with electricity and a delicious whisper of fruit. 

For Chardonnay lovers, Chablis is pure heaven (which is why some wine lovers endearingly call it “cha-bliss”). Even if you’re a staunch ABC (anything-but-Chardonnay) drinker, give Chablis a chance to change your mind. This is Chardonnay as you have never had it before.

Grand Cru

Top of the heap in taste and price are the Grand Crus. The seven south-facing vineyards cover about 101 hectares on a steep slope on the right bank of the Serein, looming over the town of Chablis. Although it may be one slope, each Grand Cru vineyard derives its own character from its steepness and exposure. Styles can range from crisp and elegant to fruity and opulent with a balancing minerality. The seven designations are as follows: Blanchot, Bougros, Les Clos, Grenouilles, Preuses, Valmur, Vaudésir. No single producer owns an entire Grand Cru vineyard—just an acre or two here or there—and it’s fascinating to taste the differences, particularly if a producer has vines in different plots.

Grand Cru wines are made to age and should rarely be enjoyed earlier than five years after release. After a decade, they’ll truly start to hit their stride. Keep an eye out for older Grand Crus in a bin sale or at a bargain price on a poorly managed wine list. Or, snag one of our picks below, and stash it in your cellar.

Louis Jadot 2021 Blanchot Grand Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

This wine displays great tension and laser-like precision, featuring notes of white peach, acacia, white mushroom, and wildflowers, all underscored by an abundance of minerality. On the palate, it offers good volume, texture, and acidity, with umami-rich fresh apples, kelp, and lady pink apples. Complex and versatile, it pairs beautifully with spiced dishes and can cellar well through 2035+. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$150 Total Wine

Domaine Laroche 2020 Les Blanchots Grand Cru “Reserve de l’Obedience” (Chablis)

For this reviewer, the delicious umami midpalate of this wine is everything. Framed by fresh verbena skin, lemon oil, acacia spice, dandelion, celery leaf and black peppercorn, the wine is exciting now but can benefit from cellaring through at least 2028. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$267 Wine Chateau

Domaine Louis Moreau 2022 Grand Cru Les Clos Chardonnay (Chablis)

Concentrated aromas of biscuit and pastry dough open on the nose along with a touch of sweet baking spice and dried hay. The palate is robust and built around a persistent fruit core that is framed by yogurt and toasted bread on the lingering finish. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$105 Saratoga Wine

Premier Cru

Premier Cru is high in quality but still widely available to consumers. Nearly 800 hectares stretch across the slopes above both banks of the Serein, as well as in some adjacent valleys. There are 40 total vineyards designated as Premier Cru based on their exposition, the slope, and the density of the Kimmeridgian chalk. However, many of their names rarely appear on wine labels.

Some of the smaller Premier Crus will often band together to produce wines under brands with greater recognition, so those 40 vineyards have essentially been reduced to 17 main sites—those are the names you’ll often see on labels. Of course, producers have the choice of using the name of the smaller Premier Cru or the larger, depending on their commercial preferences.

The Premier Cru vineyards on the right bank produce wines that are warm, rich, and powerful. Some of the most well-known vineyards on this bank are Mont de Milieu, Montée de Tonnerre, Fourchaume, and Vaucoupin. On the left bank of the Serein, Premier Crus tend to produce more restrained wines that bring out a flinty character. Vineyards to look for are Côté de Léchet, Vaillons, Montmains, Vosgros, and Vau de Vey. Enjoy most Premier Cru wines five to 10 years after release.

Domaine Billaud-Simon 2022 Montée de Tonnerre Premier Cru (Chablis)

The wine has a lively, jovial nose of white peach, Golden Delicious apple, dried field grass and yellow-pear skin. The palate is reserved, creamy and elongated with a vein of fruit enveloping plush acidity. This wine is substantial and balanced enough to be enjoyed on its own. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$79 Vins Rare

La Chablisienne 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

Alluring aromas of white peach, golden apple and candied ginger skin open on the nose and draw you into the glass. The palate is a plush amalgam of orchard fruit, brioche and honeysuckle with a persistent streak of minerality that runs throughout the length of the taste. Drink now; this wine will only continue to improve with age. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$75 Spirited Wine

William Fèvre 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

Crushed stone and green apple mingle on the nose that rests against a wall of baby’s breath and muddled white blossom. The steely palate accentuates purity of fruit while elevating freshness on the finish that is lifted by a nervy layer of acidity. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$105 Wine.com

Domaine Besson 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

This attractive bottling opens with a complex nose of muddled golden apple, wild field herbs, hay and forest floor that gathers momentum the longer you swirl it in your glass. The palate brings feathery, chalky acidity to the fore with minerality that lingers and permeates throughout and encourages the next sip. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$26 Gary’s Wine

Chablis

With over 3,700 hectares of vineyards, the classification most widely available is labeled simply “Chablis”. The wines are fruity yet balanced by a steely, mineral character that epitomizes northerly Chardonnay.

While they’re not particularly well-equipped for aging, the simple Chablis is a joy to drink young—as soon as they’re released. Instant gratification lovers, rejoice!

Domaine Laroche 2022 Vieille Voye Chardonnay (Chablis)

This wine exudes high-toned aromas of delicate wild flowers, acacia and limestone. It is lean on the palate, yet layered with vibrant notes of lemon oil, limestone, and seashells. Its complexity unfolds with each sip, offering refined balance and captivating depth. 91 Points — A.C.C.

$50 Zachy’s

Albert Bichot 2022 Domaine Long-Depaquit Chardonnay (Chablis)

Lilac, white-button mushroom and pastry aromas are accented by orange blossom on the nose. Creamy in texture, orchard fruit on the palate is lifted by a chalk-dusted undercurrent of minerality. Pair with charcuterie, fried fish and au gratin potatoes. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$44 Wine to Ship

Jean-Marc Brocard 2022 Sainte Claire Chardonnay (Chablis)

A reserved mineral-driven nose weaves in bosc pear, yellow apple and forest floor aromas that are well integrated. The relaxed palate is gently textured with lifted orchard fruit that is accented by lemon blossom and wet river stone. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$25 Ultimate Wine & Spirits

Petit Chablis

There’s one other category in the Chablis hierarchy: Petit Chablis, or “little Chablis.” Sitting on the plateau atop the region’s slopes, these Chardonnay vines are planted on Portlandian soil—a different type of chalk soil that also traces back to southern England. It’s a few hundred million years younger than its Kimmeridgian counterpart, and less rich in fossils, but don’t underestimate these wines.

Despite its meek moniker, Petit Chablis is an attractive, light, and fruity style that’s crisp and ready to drink within a few months of harvest. These wines offer an interesting and expressive Chardonnay at an accessible price.

Domaine Passy le Clou 2022 Chardonnay (Petit Chablis)

Fragrant aromas of sun-kissed yellow apple, bread dough and orange zest swirl in the glass accented by a small, bite-sized corner piece of honeyed toast. Rich in texture, the wine is well-balanced with a reserved acidity that complements the fruit core. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$32 Plum Market

J Moreau & Fils 2023 Les Petits Dieux Chardonnay (Petit Chablis)

Layered aromas of green apple, Asian pear and dried lemon peel rest on a bedrock of forest floor. The palate begins with citrus and moves into a chilled minerality that fills and lingers on the finish. Begin an evening with this wine as an aperitif. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$30 Redneck Wine Company

More Chardonnay Coverage

  • Check out our essential guide to Chardonnay.
  • Then, take a deep dive into Chardonnayspeak.
  • Or, read about the making of two 100-point California Chardonnays.
  • If you’re ready to do some more shopping, these are our favorite Chardonnays from across the globe.
  • Want to stay stateside? Here are our best California Chardonnays.
  • Or, if you’re feeling the French vibe, these white Burgundies are exceptional.
